When you do a Spark Submit to the standalone cluster, you need to constantly poll for the status. Of course, this polling can timeout due to heavy computations on the cluster. Therefore your Airflow job looks like it failed but actually the polling process failed once.
What I found is that a heavy computation job will constantly fail due to this timeout and therefore never succeed although it is completed on the cluster.

I came to the conclusion that not respecting the environment, by default, 
is not the correct behavior for this feature.
Let me explain why:

Previous versions of traitlets had not any envvar capability.
Newer versions of traitlets would support that, but client-apps would NOT get that for free; they have to add `tag(envvar='FOO')` to specific traits.  Otherwise, they will continue to behave EXACTLY as before.

Now, having by default `respect_env=False` means that for clients, modifying their tags is not enough; they must also changes all `update_config()/load_config_file()` sites.  
For instance, in [my application](https://co2mpas.io) that's in 4 places + ~20 sites in test-cod.
But why?

That's the point of a new feature: 
it should come with all hassle-free defaults, predefined.
For this PR, it is to follow the priority given in the OP of #439.

That practical point is this:
- For old clients, nothing will change.
- For those wanting the new functionality, the current defaults makes more sense.

This was probably not very clear. Hopefully better explanation: In my implementation, I was under the impression that a simple prefix code can define arbitrary symbols, as long as they are encoded with ALPHABET_SIZE bits. However, the spec clearly mentions that the integer value of a symbol must be within [0..ALPHABET_SIZE-1].

I had missed that before, thus my implementation was wrong. That allowed a corrupt stream to inject invalid block types in a prefix code. I _thought_ the underlying cause was in not rejecting invalid block types, but the real cause was in not rejecting invalid symbols in simple prefix codes.

If this `persist` is run anywhere between a pair of `id = record()` and `commit(id)` calls, it will break the commit call. What if some caller higher up the stack decides it wants to revert? It just lost the option. `persist()` has the semantics of "commit all in-progress transactions/recordings", which seems problematic anywhere we use it. I don't think we should even have the option to do that.

The more I think about it, the more I don't see a reason to allow committing multiple levels of transactions in one call:
```
record
  record
    record
commit <- I don't think this should be possible. Child transactions are in an unknown state
    commit
  commit
commit
```

Maybe we want to keep `persist` explicit, so we know when we are really writing to the wrapped database. That could make sense. Since the JournalDB implicitly starts with a record, that special outermost transaction can have a special commit: `persist`. So:

```
journal init
  record
    record
    commit
  commit
persist
```

That implies we should make the public `commit()` call fail if there's only one changeset.

` os.stat(path)` would throw a `FileNotFound` error. You'd need to either use `lstat` or pass the `follow_symlinks=False` arg (py3 only).

`lstat` gives a 41471 mode:
```python
[20]: st = os.stat(r"C:\jupyter\notebooks", follow_symlinks=False)
... : st
Out[20]: os.stat_result(st_mode=41471, st_ino=281474976710694, st_dev=113970060, st_nlink=1, st_uid=0, st_gid=0, st_size=0, st_atime=1497345137, st_mtime=1497345137, st_ctime=1497345137)

[21]: st.st_file_attributes
Out[21]: 1040
```

If we don't care about checking if a symlink is broken we could just globally use `lstat`. The only other issue is then the check for a *regular* file:
```python
if not stat.S_ISREG(st.st_mode) and not stat.S_ISDIR(st.st_mode):
    self.log.debug("%s not a regular file", os_path)
    continue
```
In that case doesn't recognise the mapped volume as a directory but sees it as a link instead:
```python
In [2]: stat.S_ISREG(41471)
Out[2]: False

In [3]: stat.S_ISDIR(41471)
Out[3]: False

In [4]: stat.S_ISLNK(41471)
Out[4]: True
```
...so the `if not WINDOWS_CONTAINER` could be replaced with `and not stat.S_ISLNK(st.st_mode)`

Responding to [this comment](https://github.com/dateutil/dateutil/pull/475#discussion_r144706735) by @jbrockmendel:

> +1 for today(tzinfo), but I'm very wary of implementing behavior that differs from same-named stdlib behavior. btw:

> ```python
> >>> tz = pytz.timezone('US/Pacific')
> >>> pd.Timestamp.today(tz)
> Timestamp('2017-10-14 17:19:04.081315-0700', tz='US/Pacific')
> ```

My reason for calling it `today` was because, frankly, I think that this is close to `datetime.datetime.today()` *should* do, and I think it violates principle of least surprise to find that `datetime.today()` is basically just an alias for `datetime.now()`. That said, the name overloading *is* my main concern about this function.

I think there are a few reasonable ways forward:

1. Leave as is because this is what `datetime.today()` *should have been* and people are more likely to be shocked that `datetime.datetime.today()` does something else than that `dateutil.utils.today()` does what it does.
2. Change the signature of this so that `utils.today()` takes an optional `time` argument in addition to an optional `tzinfo` argument. This makes it more clear that what it does is give you "today at this time, defaulting to midnight". The reason I don't like this is that there could be a conflict between `time` (which may have a `tzinfo` of its own) and `tzinfo`, and also the interface isn't nearly as clean.
3. Change the name to something like `today_midnight()` or `today_datetime()` or something.

I think I'm inclined to leave it as is, considering `pendulum` has a similar real distinction between its `now` and `today` behavior:

```python
>>> import pendulum
>>> print(pendulum.now())
2017-10-15T17:30:33.887480-04:00
>>>> print(pendulum.today())
2017-10-15T00:00:00-04:00
```

There should definitely be a way for users who run certbot with `--non-interactive` to deactivate their account. The two ways we've solved this problem in the past are to either change the default or make the user provide an extra flag like `--mass-revocation-ok`. The side effect must not happen before the the state change `ActionChannelClose` is handled, this would break Raiden's recovery guarantees. Dispatching the state change saves it to the WAL, allowing the action to be re-executed if needed on restart.

The correct approach is to dispatch the action, and let the [event handler call close](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/1f17a15c518a4af65f03c015cf13c63ef755150d/raiden/raiden_event_handler.py#L150-L178), the lock should be held individually for each channel.

On further thought... instead of using `skipif` here, I think this is a case where it's better to follow the ["EAFP principle"](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/11360858/what-is-the-eafp-principle-in-python). So we should always run the test, but wrap the call to `jedi.Script` in a `try`, like:

```python
try:
    script = jedi.Script("import trio; trio.")
except NotImplementedError:  # pragma: no cover
    pytest.skip("jedi does not yet support this version of python")
```

The advantage of doing it this way, is that it automatically works for all versions of Python – as soon as jedi starts supporting 3.8, the test will start running, and when we start testing on 3.9-dev, we won't have to adjust anything...
